Taira no Kiyomori | image = File:Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png | alt = | caption = Taira no Kiyomori | birth_name = | birth_date = {{birth date|1118|2|10}} | birth_place = {{ill|Ise Province|qid=Q864166}}; {{ill|Kyōto|qid=Q740246}} | death_date = {{death date|1181|3|20}} | death_place = {{ill|Kyōto|qid=Q740246}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}} | other_names = | occupation = {{ill|military personnel|qid=Q47064}}; {{ill|politician|qid=Q82955}}; {{ill|samurai|qid=Q38142}} | known_for = | gender= {{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}} | title= {{ill|Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin|2=ja|3=太政大臣|qid=Q1157586|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1157586}}; {{ill|Jugō|qid=Q10895712}} | spouse= {{ill|Q11410130|qid=Q11410130}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|qid=Q3379244}}; {{ill|Takashina no Motoaki's daughter|qid=Q106485436}}; {{ill|Tokiwa Gozen|qid=Q867354}} | children= {{ill|Q106536670|qid=Q106536670}}; {{ill|Q106536766|qid=Q106536766}}; {{ill|Q106549951|qid=Q106549951}}; {{ill|Q11482316|qid=Q11482316}}; {{ill|Q11482410|qid=Q11482410}}; {{ill|Q11483221|qid=Q11483221}}; {{ill|Q11483418|qid=Q11483418}}; {{ill|Q11483437|qid=Q11483437}}; {{ill|Q11486326|qid=Q11486326}}; {{ill|Taira no Munemori|qid=Q2420085}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|qid=Q710537}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|qid=Q1184985}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokuko|qid=Q3315855}}; {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|qid=Q736706}}; {{ill|Takiyasha-hime|qid=Q11565096}}; {{ill|御子姫君|qid=Q11488907}} | father= {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|qid=Q60949}}; {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|qid=Q3083805}} | mother= {{ill|sister of Gion-nyōgo|qid=Q106448499}} | family= {{ill|Ise Heishi|qid=Q15734686}} | religion= {{ill|Buddhism|qid=Q748}} | native_name= 平清盛 | given_name= {{ill|Kiyomori|qid=Q123817191}} | resting_place= {{ill|Nōfuku-ji|qid=Q11612054}} }} [[Image:Taira no Kiyomori.jpg|thumb|right|240px|Taira no Kiyomori in his later years, in book illustration by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]]]] [[File:Taira no Kiyomori kao.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Taira no Kiyomori's signature (''[[:en:Kaō|Kaō]]'').]] {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Taira no Kiyomori'''|平 清盛||1118 – March 20, 1181}} was a military leader and ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' of the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. He established the first [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]-dominated administrative government in the [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]]. ==Early life== Kiyomori was born in Japan, in 1118 as the first son of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]. His mother, Gion no Nyogo, was wife of Tadamori and a palace servant according to ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. == Family == '''Father''': [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]
'''Mother''': Gion no Nyogo (d. 1147)
'''Concubine(s)''': [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]]
'''Children''': * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Taira no Munemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]] * [[Taira no Tokuko]] * [[:en:Taira no Shigehira|Taira no Shigehira]] ==Career== After the death of his father in 1153, Kiyomori assumed control of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan and ambitiously entered the political realm, in which he had previously only held a minor post. Before that though, in 1156, he and [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan, suppressed the rebels in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]]. This established the Taira and Minamoto as the top samurai clans in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, this caused the allies to become bitter rivals which culminated four years later during the [[:en:Heiji rebellion|Heiji rebellion]] in 1160. Kiyomori, emerging victorious with Yoshitomo (whose two eldest sons were killed), was now the head of the single most powerful warrior clan in imperial capital [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, his clan's power and influence in the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|provinces]] at this time is a matter of debate. Kiyomori showed mercy and exiled a few of Yoshitomo's surviving sons, including [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]], [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Noriyori]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] – a benevolence that would turn out to be the Taira clan's downfall later on.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=256–259}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |page=111}} Due to his status as the head of the sole remaining warrior clan in imperial court, Kiyomori was in a unique position to manipulate the court rivalry between the [[:en:Cloistered Emperor|retired emperor]], [[:en:Go-Shirakawa|Go-Shirakawa]], and his son, [[:en:Emperor Nijō|Emperor Nijō]]. Because of this manipulation, Kiyomori was able to climb the Ranks of government, though the majority of his promotions as well as the success of his family in gaining Ranks and titles at court was due to Shirakawa's patronage. This culminated in 1167, when Kiyomori became the first [[:en:Courtier|Courtier]] of a warrior family to be appointed ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', chief minister of the government, and the ''[[:en:De facto|De facto]]'' administrator of the imperial government. As was the norm, he soon relinquished the position and leadership of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan, with the goal of maintaining the social and political prestige of having attained the highest office in the land, but being free of the attendant duties. This had been a common practice for many years in the highest levels of ancient Japanese government and in doing so Kiyomori was asserting what he felt was his strong position in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Kyoto government]]. However, many of the courtiers from traditional civil (non-warrior) noble families were less than pleased with both Kiyomori's attainment of power, and how he comported himself with regard to other high Ranking courtiers.{{rp|266–267}} [[Image:Yoshitoshi The Fever.jpg|thumb|350px|right|While suffering from a [[:en:fever|fever]], Taira no Kiyomori is confronted by a vision of hell and the ghosts of his victims, in an 1883 print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]].]] In 1171, Kiyomori arranged a marriage between his daughter [[Taira no Tokuko|Tokuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]]. Their first son, the future [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]], was born in 1178.{{rp|268,285}} The next year, in 1179, Kiyomori staged a [[:en:Coup d'état|Coup d'état]] forcing the resignation of his rivals from all government posts and subsequently banishing them. He then filled the open government positions with his allies and relatives, and imprisoned (house arrest) the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. Finally, in 1180 Kiyomori forced Emperor Takakura to abdicate and give Prince Tokihito the throne, who then became [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]].{{rp|275,285}} With the exertion of Taira power and wealth and Kiyomori's new monopoly on authority, many of his allies, most of the provincial samurai, and even members of his own clan turned against him. [[:en:Prince Mochihito|Prince Mochihito]], brother of [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]], called on Kiyomori's old rivals of the Minamoto clan to rise against the Taira, beginning the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] in the middle of 1180. Kiyomori died early the next year from sickness, leaving his son [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Munemori]] to preside over the downfall and destruction of the Taira at the hands of the Minamoto in 1185.{{rp|278,287}} ''The Tale of the Heike'' states that as he lay dying, Kiyomori's fever was so high that anyone who attempted to even get near him would be burned by the heat.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=9780231138031 |page=66}} [[File:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|thumb|upright=0.85|left|[[Taira no Kiyomori]] encounters the fox goddess Kiko Tennō ([[Dakini|Dakiniten]]), by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]]] The rapid rise of certain notable figures to prominence, as well as their decline, have been popularly attributed to Dakiniten. A certain anecdote regarding the military leader [[Taira no Kiyomori]] found in the ''[[:en:Genpei Jōsuiki|Genpei Jōsuiki]]'' (one of a number of variants of the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'') claims that Kiyomori once shot an arrow at a fox during a hunt. The fox then transformed into a woman who promised to grant Kiyomori whatever he wanted in exchange for her life. Kiyomori, realizing this woman is none other than the goddess Kiko Tennō (貴狐天王, lit. "Venerable Fox [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]-King", i.e. Dakiniten), spared her life. He subsequently became a devotee of the goddess, despite his awareness that the benefits obtained through the Dakiniten rite (吒天の法, ''Daten no hō'') would not be passed on to his progeny. The story thus attributes both Kiyomori's rise in power and the subsequent fall of [[Taira clan|his clan]] to his performance of the Dakiniten ritual.{{sfnp|Faure|2015b|pp=121, 224–225}}{{sfnp|Bathgate|2004|p=154}}{{sfnp|Smyers|1999|p=84}} ==Cultural references== Taira no Kiyomori is the main character in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] epic, the ''[[:en:Tale of Heike|Tale of Heike]]''. The [[:en:Daiei Film|Daiei Film]] production of [[:en:Kenji Mizoguchi|Kenji Mizoguchi]]'s 1955 film ''[[:en:Shin Heike Monogatari (film)|Shin Heike Monogatari]]'' (variously translated as ''[[Taira clan|Taira Clan]] Saga, Tales of the Taira Clan'', and ''The Sacrilegious Hero'') credits its story as "from the novel by [[:en:Yoshikawa Eiji|Yoshikawa Eiji]]", which in turn is a 1950 retelling of the 14th-century epic ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. The opening introduction to the film, in its English subtitles, is {{quote|Japan, in the Tenth and Eleventh centuries, was virtually controlled by the [[Fujiwara clan]]. But in the Twelfth century, Fujiwara influence began to wane, partly due to the double-monarchy. An Emperor would abdicate but continue to rule from behind the scenes. Thus there was an Imperial Court and an ex-Emperor's Cloister Court, both emperors being descended from the Sun Goddess. Inevitably, there was conflict between the courts. Both began to depend on the warriors, the [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]. Until then, Fujiwara rule had involved little bloodshed. Some monasteries also had their own armies. The monasteries used them to intimidate both courts. Big landowners paid no taxes. Piracy and banditry increased. The Cloister Court attempted to restore order using the warriors of the samurai Taira Clan. Thus were sown the seeds of military governments which dominated Japan for 700 years ''[i.e., [[:en:Meiji era|until 1868]]]''. This story begins in 1137, in Kyoto, ancient capital of Japan.}} Unlike most other tellings, Mizoguchi's film includes only the story of Taira no Kiyomori's youth, depicting him as a [[:en:Hero|heroic character]], particularly in breaking the power of the tyrannical armed monks and their [[Palanquin#Japan|palanquin shrines]], where he says at his father's grave "Father, with two arrows from my bow I destroyed a superstition that gripped men for centuries. The courtiers and priests have tried to have me for blasphemy. But others have supported me, more than I expected. Some of them are lords, too. Father, a greater battle lies ahead. But I remain undaunted. No matter how I am beaten, I shall rise again". The film then ends with Kiyomori approaching an alfresco Fujiwara dance, vowing to himself, "Dance, my Lords, dance. Your end is near. Tomorrow will be ours!"DVD ''Le héros sacrilège'', Films sans Frontières 2004 Taira no Kiyomori was featured by 19th-century [[:en:Woodblock printing in Japan|woodblock print artists]] as an exemplar of guilt and retribution, see the accompanying print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]. The famous print generally known as ''The Vision of Kiyomori'' by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] depicts the actor [[Nakamura Utayemon IV]] in the character of Kiyomori, confronted by the horrific vision of his snow-filled garden transformed into the heaped bones and skulls of his slaughtered enemies. In video games, Kiyomori appears in [[:en:Warriors Orochi|Warriors Orochi]] 2, 3 and 4 fighting for Orochi's army and using prayer beads as weapons. He also makes an appearance in ''[[:en:Dynasty Warriors: Strikeforce|Dynasty Warriors Strikeforce]]'', as a boss in one of the game's Crossover Missions. Additionally, he is the main antagonist in ''[[:en:Harukanaru Toki no Naka de 3|Harukanaru Toki no Naka De 3]]''. Kiyomori also features prominently as a sympathetic villain in [[:en:Osamu Tezuka|Osamu Tezuka]]'s [[:en:Phoenix (manga)|Phoenix series]] in the first half of the ninth volume, ''Turbulent Times'' (retitled ''Civil War'' in English), another [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] epic. Like most villains in the series he desires the titular bird for its immortality granting blood, due to his desire to continue to lead and protect the Taira clan and lack of confidence in his successors, but winds up being tricked into buying an imported [[:en:peacock|peacock]] instead. A character named Lord Kiyomori appears in Book 6, "The Lords of the Rising Sun" in the [[:en:Fabled Lands|Fabled Lands]] adventure gamebook series, where he is portrayed as the Imperial Sovereign Takakura's chancellor, and on the verge of war with the self-proclaimed shogun by the name of "Yoritomo". The 2012 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] was about him.{{Cite news|title=大河ドラマ「Taira no Kiyomori」：語り部は敵方・源頼朝 イケメンをキャスティング予定|work=まんたんウェブ|publisher=毎日新聞デジタル|date=2011-05-12|url=http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110513031118/http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-date=2011-05-13 |access-date=2021-01-27}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Junior First Rank]] (11 February 1167) ==See also== * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Shishigatani incident|Shishigatani incident]] * [[:en:Fukuhara-kyō|Fukuhara-kyō]] * [[Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Siege of Shirakawa-den|Siege of Shirakawa-den]] ==References== |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5772-1}} * {{cite book |last=Smyers |first=Karen Ann |author-link=Karen Ann Smyers |title=The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship |year=1999 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-2058-9}} {{Daijō-daijin}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:1181 deaths]] [[Category:Taira clan]] {{ill|Q11483437|qid=Q11483437}}; {{ill|Q11486326|qid=Q11486326}}; {{ill|Taira no Munemori|qid=Q2420085}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|qid=Q710537}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|qid=Q1184985}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokuko|qid=Q3315855}}; {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|qid=Q736706}}; {{ill|Takiyasha-hime|qid=Q11565096}}; {{ill|御子姫君|qid=Q11488907}} | father= {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|qid=Q60949}}; {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|qid=Q3083805}} | mother= {{ill|sister of Gion-nyōgo|qid=Q106448499}} | family= {{ill|Ise Heishi|qid=Q15734686}} | religion= {{ill|Buddhism|qid=Q748}} | native_name= 平清盛 | given_name= {{ill|Kiyomori|qid=Q123817191}} | resting_place= {{ill|Nōfuku-ji|qid=Q11612054}} }} [[Image:Taira no Kiyomori.jpg|thumb|right|240px|Taira no Kiyomori in his later years, in book illustration by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]]]] [[File:Taira no Kiyomori kao.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Taira no Kiyomori's signature (''[[:en:Kaō|Kaō]]'').]] {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Taira no Kiyomori'''|平 清盛||1118 – March 20, 1181}} was a military leader and ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' of the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. He established the first [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]-dominated administrative government in the [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]]. ==Early life== Kiyomori was born in Japan, in 1118 as the first son of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]. His mother, Gion no Nyogo, was wife of Tadamori and a palace servant according to ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. == Family == '''Father''': [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]
'''Mother''': Gion no Nyogo (d. 1147)
'''Concubine(s)''': [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]]
'''Children''': * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Taira no Munemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]] * [[Taira no Tokuko]] * [[:en:Taira no Shigehira|Taira no Shigehira]] ==Career== After the death of his father in 1153, Kiyomori assumed control of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan and ambitiously entered the political realm, in which he had previously only held a minor post. Before that though, in 1156, he and [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan, suppressed the rebels in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]]. This established the Taira and Minamoto as the top samurai clans in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, this caused the allies to become bitter rivals which culminated four years later during the [[:en:Heiji rebellion|Heiji rebellion]] in 1160. Kiyomori, emerging victorious with Yoshitomo (whose two eldest sons were killed), was now the head of the single most powerful warrior clan in imperial capital [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, his clan's power and influence in the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|provinces]] at this time is a matter of debate. Kiyomori showed mercy and exiled a few of Yoshitomo's surviving sons, including [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]], [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Noriyori]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] – a benevolence that would turn out to be the Taira clan's downfall later on.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=256–259}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |page=111}} Due to his status as the head of the sole remaining warrior clan in imperial court, Kiyomori was in a unique position to manipulate the court rivalry between the [[:en:Cloistered Emperor|retired emperor]], [[:en:Go-Shirakawa|Go-Shirakawa]], and his son, [[:en:Emperor Nijō|Emperor Nijō]]. Because of this manipulation, Kiyomori was able to climb the Ranks of government, though the majority of his promotions as well as the success of his family in gaining Ranks and titles at court was due to Shirakawa's patronage. This culminated in 1167, when Kiyomori became the first [[:en:Courtier|Courtier]] of a warrior family to be appointed ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', chief minister of the government, and the ''[[:en:De facto|De facto]]'' administrator of the imperial government. As was the norm, he soon relinquished the position and leadership of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan, with the goal of maintaining the social and political prestige of having attained the highest office in the land, but being free of the attendant duties. This had been a common practice for many years in the highest levels of ancient Japanese government and in doing so Kiyomori was asserting what he felt was his strong position in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Kyoto government]]. However, many of the courtiers from traditional civil (non-warrior) noble families were less than pleased with both Kiyomori's attainment of power, and how he comported himself with regard to other high Ranking courtiers.{{rp|266–267}} [[Image:Yoshitoshi The Fever.jpg|thumb|350px|right|While suffering from a [[:en:fever|fever]], Taira no Kiyomori is confronted by a vision of hell and the ghosts of his victims, in an 1883 print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]].]] In 1171, Kiyomori arranged a marriage between his daughter [[Taira no Tokuko|Tokuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]]. Their first son, the future [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]], was born in 1178.{{rp|268,285}} The next year, in 1179, Kiyomori staged a [[:en:Coup d'état|Coup d'état]] forcing the resignation of his rivals from all government posts and subsequently banishing them. He then filled the open government positions with his allies and relatives, and imprisoned (house arrest) the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. Finally, in 1180 Kiyomori forced Emperor Takakura to abdicate and give Prince Tokihito the throne, who then became [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]].{{rp|275,285}} With the exertion of Taira power and wealth and Kiyomori's new monopoly on authority, many of his allies, most of the provincial samurai, and even members of his own clan turned against him. [[:en:Prince Mochihito|Prince Mochihito]], brother of [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]], called on Kiyomori's old rivals of the Minamoto clan to rise against the Taira, beginning the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] in the middle of 1180. Kiyomori died early the next year from sickness, leaving his son [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Munemori]] to preside over the downfall and destruction of the Taira at the hands of the Minamoto in 1185.{{rp|278,287}} ''The Tale of the Heike'' states that as he lay dying, Kiyomori's fever was so high that anyone who attempted to even get near him would be burned by the heat.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=9780231138031 |page=66}} [[File:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|thumb|upright=0.85|left|[[Taira no Kiyomori]] encounters the fox goddess Kiko Tennō ([[Dakini|Dakiniten]]), by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]]] The rapid rise of certain notable figures to prominence, as well as their decline, have been popularly attributed to Dakiniten. A certain anecdote regarding the military leader [[Taira no Kiyomori]] found in the ''[[:en:Genpei Jōsuiki|Genpei Jōsuiki]]'' (one of a number of variants of the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'') claims that Kiyomori once shot an arrow at a fox during a hunt. The fox then transformed into a woman who promised to grant Kiyomori whatever he wanted in exchange for her life. Kiyomori, realizing this woman is none other than the goddess Kiko Tennō (貴狐天王, lit. "Venerable Fox [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]-King", i.e. Dakiniten), spared her life. He subsequently became a devotee of the goddess, despite his awareness that the benefits obtained through the Dakiniten rite (吒天の法, ''Daten no hō'') would not be passed on to his progeny. The story thus attributes both Kiyomori's rise in power and the subsequent fall of [[Taira clan|his clan]] to his performance of the Dakiniten ritual.{{sfnp|Faure|2015b|pp=121, 224–225}}{{sfnp|Bathgate|2004|p=154}}{{sfnp|Smyers|1999|p=84}} ==Cultural references== Taira no Kiyomori is the main character in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] epic, the ''[[:en:Tale of Heike|Tale of Heike]]''. The [[:en:Daiei Film|Daiei Film]] production of [[:en:Kenji Mizoguchi|Kenji Mizoguchi]]'s 1955 film ''[[:en:Shin Heike Monogatari (film)|Shin Heike Monogatari]]'' (variously translated as ''[[Taira clan|Taira Clan]] Saga, Tales of the Taira Clan'', and ''The Sacrilegious Hero'') credits its story as "from the novel by [[:en:Yoshikawa Eiji|Yoshikawa Eiji]]", which in turn is a 1950 retelling of the 14th-century epic ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. The opening introduction to the film, in its English subtitles, is {{quote|Japan, in the Tenth and Eleventh centuries, was virtually controlled by the [[Fujiwara clan]]. But in the Twelfth century, Fujiwara influence began to wane, partly due to the double-monarchy. An Emperor would abdicate but continue to rule from behind the scenes. Thus there was an Imperial Court and an ex-Emperor's Cloister Court, both emperors being descended from the Sun Goddess. Inevitably, there was conflict between the courts. Both began to depend on the warriors, the [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]. Until then, Fujiwara rule had involved little bloodshed. Some monasteries also had their own armies. The monasteries used them to intimidate both courts. Big landowners paid no taxes. Piracy and banditry increased. The Cloister Court attempted to restore order using the warriors of the samurai Taira Clan. Thus were sown the seeds of military governments which dominated Japan for 700 years ''[i.e., [[:en:Meiji era|until 1868]]]''. This story begins in 1137, in Kyoto, ancient capital of Japan.}} Unlike most other tellings, Mizoguchi's film includes only the story of Taira no Kiyomori's youth, depicting him as a [[:en:Hero|heroic character]], particularly in breaking the power of the tyrannical armed monks and their [[Palanquin#Japan|palanquin shrines]], where he says at his father's grave "Father, with two arrows from my bow I destroyed a superstition that gripped men for centuries. The courtiers and priests have tried to have me for blasphemy. But others have supported me, more than I expected. Some of them are lords, too. Father, a greater battle lies ahead. But I remain undaunted. No matter how I am beaten, I shall rise again". The film then ends with Kiyomori approaching an alfresco Fujiwara dance, vowing to himself, "Dance, my Lords, dance. Your end is near. Tomorrow will be ours!"DVD ''Le héros sacrilège'', Films sans Frontières 2004 Taira no Kiyomori was featured by 19th-century [[:en:Woodblock printing in Japan|woodblock print artists]] as an exemplar of guilt and retribution, see the accompanying print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]. The famous print generally known as ''The Vision of Kiyomori'' by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] depicts the actor [[Nakamura Utayemon IV]] in the character of Kiyomori, confronted by the horrific vision of his snow-filled garden transformed into the heaped bones and skulls of his slaughtered enemies. In video games, Kiyomori appears in [[:en:Warriors Orochi|Warriors Orochi]] 2, 3 and 4 fighting for Orochi's army and using prayer beads as weapons. He also makes an appearance in ''[[:en:Dynasty Warriors: Strikeforce|Dynasty Warriors Strikeforce]]'', as a boss in one of the game's Crossover Missions. Additionally, he is the main antagonist in ''[[:en:Harukanaru Toki no Naka de 3|Harukanaru Toki no Naka De 3]]''. Kiyomori also features prominently as a sympathetic villain in [[:en:Osamu Tezuka|Osamu Tezuka]]'s [[:en:Phoenix (manga)|Phoenix series]] in the first half of the ninth volume, ''Turbulent Times'' (retitled ''Civil War'' in English), another [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] epic. Like most villains in the series he desires the titular bird for its immortality granting blood, due to his desire to continue to lead and protect the Taira clan and lack of confidence in his successors, but winds up being tricked into buying an imported [[:en:peacock|peacock]] instead. A character named Lord Kiyomori appears in Book 6, "The Lords of the Rising Sun" in the [[:en:Fabled Lands|Fabled Lands]] adventure gamebook series, where he is portrayed as the Imperial Sovereign Takakura's chancellor, and on the verge of war with the self-proclaimed shogun by the name of "Yoritomo". The 2012 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] was about him.{{Cite news|title=大河ドラマ「Taira no Kiyomori」：語り部は敵方・源頼朝 イケメンをキャスティング予定|work=まんたんウェブ|publisher=毎日新聞デジタル|date=2011-05-12|url=http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110513031118/http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-date=2011-05-13 |access-date=2021-01-27}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Junior First Rank]] (11 February 1167) ==See also== * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Shishigatani incident|Shishigatani incident]] * [[:en:Fukuhara-kyō|Fukuhara-kyō]] * [[Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Siege of Shirakawa-den|Siege of Shirakawa-den]] ==References== |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5772-1}} * {{cite book |last=Smyers |first=Karen Ann |author-link=Karen Ann Smyers |title=The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in '''Mother''': Gion no Nyogo (d. 1147)
'''Concubine(s)''': [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]]
'''Children''': * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Taira no Munemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]] * [[Taira no Tokuko]] * [[:en:Taira no Shigehira|Taira no Shigehira]] ==Career== After the death of his father in 1153, Kiyomori assumed control of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan and ambitiously entered the political realm, in which he had previously only held a minor post. Before that though, in 1156, he and [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan, suppressed the rebels in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]]. This established the Taira and Minamoto as the top samurai clans in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, this caused the allies to become bitter rivals which culminated four years later during the [[:en:Heiji rebellion|Heiji rebellion]] in 1160. Kiyomori, emerging victorious with Yoshitomo (whose two eldest sons were killed), was now the head of the single most powerful warrior clan in imperial capital [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, his clan's power and influence in the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|provinces]] at this time is a matter of debate. Kiyomori showed mercy and exiled a few of Yoshitomo's surviving sons, including [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]], [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Noriyori]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] – a benevolence that would turn out to be the Taira clan's downfall later on.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=256–259}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |page=111}} Due to his status as the head of the sole remaining warrior clan in imperial court, Kiyomori was in a unique position to manipulate the court rivalry between the [[:en:Cloistered Emperor|retired emperor]], [[:en:Go-Shirakawa|Go-Shirakawa]], and his son, [[:en:Emperor Nijō|Emperor Nijō]]. Because of this manipulation, Kiyomori was able to climb the Ranks of government, though the majority of his promotions as well as the success of his family in gaining Ranks and titles at court was due to Shirakawa's patronage. This culminated in 1167, when Kiyomori became the first [[:en:Courtier|Courtier]] of a warrior family to be appointed ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', chief minister of the government, and the ''[[:en:De facto|De facto]]'' administrator of the imperial government. As was the norm, he soon relinquished the position and leadership of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan, with the goal of maintaining the social and political prestige of having attained the highest office in the land, but being free of the attendant duties. This had been a common practice for many years in the highest levels of ancient Japanese government and in doing so Kiyomori was asserting what he felt was his strong position in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Kyoto government]]. However, many of the courtiers from traditional civil (non-warrior) noble families were less than pleased with both Kiyomori's attainment of power, and how he comported himself with regard to other high Ranking courtiers.{{rp|266–267}} [[Image:Yoshitoshi The Fever.jpg|thumb|350px|right|While suffering from a [[:en:fever|fever]], Taira no Kiyomori is confronted by a vision of hell and the ghosts of his victims, in an 1883 print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]].]] In 1171, Kiyomori arranged a marriage between his daughter [[Taira no Tokuko|Tokuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]]. Their first son, the future [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]], was born in 1178.{{rp|268,285}} The next year, in 1179, Kiyomori staged a [[:en:Coup d'état|Coup d'état]] forcing the resignation of his rivals from all government posts and subsequently banishing them. He then filled the open government positions with his allies and relatives, and imprisoned (house arrest) the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. Finally, in 1180 Kiyomori forced Emperor Takakura to abdicate and give Prince Tokihito the throne, who then became [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]].{{rp|275,285}} With the exertion of Taira power and wealth and Kiyomori's new monopoly on authority, many of his allies, most of the provincial samurai, and even members of his own clan turned against him. [[:en:Prince Mochihito|Prince Mochihito]], brother of [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]], called on Kiyomori's old rivals of the Minamoto clan to rise against the Taira, beginning the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] in the middle of 1180. Kiyomori died early the next year from sickness, leaving his son [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Munemori]] to preside over the downfall and destruction of the Taira at the hands of the Minamoto in 1185.{{rp|278,287}} ''The Tale of the Heike'' states that as he lay dying, Kiyomori's fever was so high that anyone who attempted to even get near him would be burned by the heat.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=9780231138031 |page=66}} [[File:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|thumb|upright=0.85|left|[[Taira no Kiyomori]] encounters the fox goddess Kiko Tennō ([[Dakini|Dakiniten]]), by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]]] The rapid rise of certain notable figures to prominence, as well as their decline, have been popularly attributed to Dakiniten. A certain anecdote regarding the military leader [[Taira no Kiyomori]] found in the ''[[:en:Genpei Jōsuiki|Genpei Jōsuiki]]'' (one of a number of variants of the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'') claims that Kiyomori once shot an arrow at a fox during a hunt. The fox then transformed into a woman who promised to grant Kiyomori whatever he wanted in exchange for her life. Kiyomori, realizing this woman is none other than the goddess Kiko Tennō (貴狐天王, lit. "Venerable Fox [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]-King", i.e. Dakiniten), spared her life. He subsequently became a devotee of the goddess, despite his awareness that the benefits obtained through the Dakiniten rite (吒天の法, ''Daten no hō'') would not be passed on to his progeny. The story thus attributes both Kiyomori's rise in power and the subsequent fall of [[Taira clan|his clan]] to his performance of the Dakiniten ritual.{{sfnp|Faure|2015b|pp=121, 224–225}}{{sfnp|Bathgate|2004|p=154}}{{sfnp|Smyers|1999|p=84}} ==Cultural references== Taira no Kiyomori is the main character in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] epic, the ''[[:en:Tale of Heike|Tale of Heike]]''. The [[:en:Daiei Film|Daiei Film]] production of [[:en:Kenji Mizoguchi|Kenji Mizoguchi]]'s 1955 film ''[[:en:Shin Heike Monogatari (film)|Shin Heike Monogatari]]'' (variously translated as ''[[Taira clan|Taira Clan]] Saga, Tales of the Taira Clan'', and ''The Sacrilegious Hero'') credits its story as "from the novel by [[:en:Yoshikawa Eiji|Yoshikawa Eiji]]", which in turn is a 1950 retelling of the 14th-century epic ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. The opening introduction to the film, in its English subtitles, is {{quote|Japan, in the Tenth and Eleventh centuries, was virtually controlled by the [[Fujiwara clan]]. But in the Twelfth century, Fujiwara influence began to wane, partly due to the double-monarchy. An Emperor would abdicate but continue to rule from behind the scenes. Thus there was an Imperial Court and an ex-Emperor's Cloister Court, both emperors being descended from the Sun Goddess. Inevitably, there was conflict between the courts. Both began to depend on the warriors, the [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]. Until then, Fujiwara rule had involved little bloodshed. Some monasteries also had their own armies. The monasteries used them to intimidate both courts. Big landowners paid no taxes. Piracy and banditry increased. The Cloister Court attempted to restore order using the warriors of the samurai Taira Clan. Thus were sown the seeds of military governments which dominated Japan for 700 years ''[i.e., [[:en:Meiji era|until 1868]]]''. This story begins in 1137, in Kyoto, ancient capital of Japan.}} Unlike most other tellings, Mizoguchi's film includes only the story of Taira no Kiyomori's youth, depicting him as a [[:en:Hero|heroic character]], particularly in breaking the power of the tyrannical armed monks and their [[Palanquin#Japan|palanquin shrines]], where he says at his father's grave "Father, with two arrows from my bow I destroyed a superstition that gripped men for centuries. The courtiers and priests have tried to have me for blasphemy. But others have supported me, more than I expected. Some of them are lords, too. Father, a greater battle lies ahead. But I remain undaunted. No matter how I am beaten, I shall rise again". The film then ends with Kiyomori approaching an alfresco Fujiwara dance, vowing to himself, "Dance, my Lords, dance. Your end is near. Tomorrow will be ours!"DVD ''Le héros sacrilège'', Films sans Frontières 2004 Taira no Kiyomori was featured by 19th-century [[:en:Woodblock printing in Japan|woodblock print artists]] as an exemplar of guilt and retribution, see the accompanying print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]. The famous print generally known as ''The Vision of Kiyomori'' by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] depicts the actor [[Nakamura Utayemon IV]] in the character of Kiyomori, confronted by the horrific vision of his snow-filled garden transformed into the heaped bones and skulls of his slaughtered enemies. In video games, Kiyomori appears in [[:en:Warriors Orochi|Warriors Orochi]] 2, 3 and 4 fighting for Orochi's army and using prayer beads as weapons. He also makes an appearance in ''[[:en:Dynasty Warriors: Strikeforce|Dynasty Warriors Strikeforce]]'', as a boss in one of the game's Crossover Missions. Additionally, he is the main antagonist in ''[[:en:Harukanaru Toki no Naka de 3|Harukanaru Toki no Naka De 3]]''. Kiyomori also features prominently as a sympathetic villain in [[:en:Osamu Tezuka|Osamu Tezuka]]'s [[:en:Phoenix (manga)|Phoenix series]] in the first half of the ninth volume, ''Turbulent Times'' (retitled ''Civil War'' in English), another [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] epic. Like most villains in the series he desires the titular bird for its immortality granting blood, due to his desire to continue to lead and protect the Taira clan and lack of confidence in his successors, but winds up being tricked into buying an imported [[:en:peacock|peacock]] instead. A character named Lord Kiyomori appears in Book 6, "The Lords of the Rising Sun" in the [[:en:Fabled Lands|Fabled Lands]] adventure gamebook series, where he is portrayed as the Imperial Sovereign Takakura's chancellor, and on the verge of war with the self-proclaimed shogun by the name of "Yoritomo". The 2012 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] was about him.{{Cite news|title=大河ドラマ「Taira no Kiyomori」：語り部は敵方・源頼朝 イケメンをキャスティング予定|work=まんたんウェブ|publisher=毎日新聞デジタル|date=2011-05-12|url=http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110513031118/http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-date=2011-05-13 |access-date=2021-01-27}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Junior First Rank]] (11 February 1167) ==See also== * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Shishigatani incident|Shishigatani incident]] * [[:en:Fukuhara-kyō|Fukuhara-kyō]] * [[Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Siege of Shirakawa-den|Siege of Shirakawa-den]] ==References== {{Commonscat|Taira no Kiyomori}} {{reflist}} ==Sources== * {{cite book |last1=Bathgate |first1=Michael |title=The Fox's Craft in Japanese Religion and Culture: Shapeshifters, Transformations, and Duplicities |date=2004 |publisher=Taylor & Francis |isbn=978-1-135-88391-1}} * {{cite book |last1=Faure |first1=Bernard |title=Protectors and Predators: Gods of Medieval Japan, Volume 2 |date=2015b |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5772-1}} * {{cite book |last=Smyers |first=Karen Ann |author-link=Karen Ann Smyers |title=The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship |year=1999 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-2058-9}} {{Daijō-daijin}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:1181 deaths]] [[Category:Taira clan]] [[Category:People of Heian-period Japan]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist clergy]] [[Category:12th-century Buddhist monks]] [[Category:People of the Genpei War]] Kiyomori]] [[ca:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[cs:Kijomori Taira]] [[de:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[es:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[eu:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[fa:تایرا نو کیوموری]] [[fr:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[hak:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[he:טאירה נו קיומורי]] [[hu:Taira no Kijomori]] [[id:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[it:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[ja:平清盛]] [[jv:Taira no Kiyomori]] [[ko:다이라노 기요모리]] [[nl:Taira no Kiyomori]] {{ill|Kyōto|WD=Q740246}} | death_date = {{death date|1181|3|20}} | death_place = {{ill|Kyōto|WD=Q740246}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | other_names = | occupation = {{ill|military personnel|WD=Q47064}}; {{ill|Jugō|WD=Q10895712}} | spouse= {{ill|Q11410130|WD=Q11410130}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244}}; {{ill|Takashina no Motoaki's daughter|WD=Q106485436}}; {{ill|Tokiwa Gozen|WD=Q867354}} | children= {{ill|Q106536670|WD=Q106536670}}; {{ill|Takiyasha-hime|WD=Q11565096}}; {{ill|御子姫君|WD=Q11488907}} | father= {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|WD=Q60949}}; {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|WD=Q3083805}} | mother= {{ill|sister of Gion-nyōgo|WD=Q106448499}} | family= {{ill|Ise Heishi|WD=Q15734686}} | religion= {{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}} | native_name= 平清盛 | given_name= {{ill|Kiyomori|WD=Q123817191}} | resting_place= {{ill|Nōfuku-ji|WD=Q11612054}} }} [[Image:Taira no Kiyomori.jpg|thumb|right|240px|Taira no Kiyomori in his later years, in book illustration by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]]]] [[File:Taira no Kiyomori kao.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Taira no Kiyomori's signature (''[[:en:Kaō|Kaō]]'').]] {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Taira no Kiyomori'''|平 清盛||1118 – March 20, 1181}} was a military leader and ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' of the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. He established the first [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]-dominated administrative government in the [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]]. ==Early life== Kiyomori was born in Japan, in 1118 as the first son of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]. His mother, Gion no Nyogo, was wife of Tadamori and a palace servant according to ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. == Family == '''Father''': [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]
'''Mother''': Gion no Nyogo (d. 1147)
'''Concubine(s)''': [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]]
'''Children''': * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Taira no Munemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]] * [[Taira no Tokuko]] * [[:en:Taira no Shigehira|Taira no Shigehira]] ==Career== After the death of his father in 1153, Kiyomori assumed control of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan and ambitiously entered the political realm, in which he had previously only held a minor post. Before that though, in 1156, he and [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan, suppressed the rebels in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]]. This established the Taira and Minamoto as the top samurai clans in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, this caused the allies to become bitter rivals which culminated four years later during the [[:en:Heiji rebellion|Heiji rebellion]] in 1160. Kiyomori, emerging victorious with Yoshitomo (whose two eldest sons were killed), was now the head of the single most powerful warrior clan in imperial capital [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, his clan's power and influence in the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|provinces]] at this time is a matter of debate. Kiyomori showed mercy and exiled a few of Yoshitomo's surviving sons, including [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]], [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Noriyori]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] – a benevolence that would turn out to be the Taira clan's downfall later on.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=256–259}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |page=111}} Due to his status as the head of the sole remaining warrior clan in imperial court, Kiyomori was in a unique position to manipulate the court rivalry between the [[:en:Cloistered Emperor|retired emperor]], [[:en:Go-Shirakawa|Go-Shirakawa]], and his son, [[:en:Emperor Nijō|Emperor Nijō]]. Because of this manipulation, Kiyomori was able to climb the Ranks of government, though the majority of his promotions as well as the success of his family in gaining Ranks and titles at court was due to Shirakawa's patronage. This culminated in 1167, when Kiyomori became the first [[:en:Courtier|Courtier]] of a warrior family to be appointed ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', chief minister of the government, and the ''[[:en:De facto|De facto]]'' administrator of the imperial government. As was the norm, he soon relinquished the position and leadership of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan, with the goal of maintaining the social and political prestige of having attained the highest office in the land, but being free of the attendant duties. This had been a common practice for many years in the highest levels of ancient Japanese government and in doing so Kiyomori was asserting what he felt was his strong position in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Kyoto government]]. However, many of the courtiers from traditional civil (non-warrior) noble families were less than pleased with both Kiyomori's attainment of power, and how he comported himself with regard to other high Ranking courtiers.{{rp|266–267}} [[Image:Yoshitoshi The Fever.jpg|thumb|350px|right|While suffering from a [[:en:fever|fever]], Taira no Kiyomori is confronted by a vision of hell and the ghosts of his victims, in an 1883 print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]].]] In 1171, Kiyomori arranged a marriage between his daughter [[Taira no Tokuko|Tokuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]]. Their first son, the future [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]], was born in 1178.{{rp|268,285}} The next year, in 1179, Kiyomori staged a [[:en:Coup d'état|Coup d'état]] forcing the resignation of his rivals from all government posts and subsequently banishing them. He then filled the open government positions with his allies and relatives, and imprisoned (house arrest) the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. Finally, in 1180 Kiyomori forced Emperor Takakura to abdicate and give Prince Tokihito the throne, who then became [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]].{{rp|275,285}} With the exertion of Taira power and wealth and Kiyomori's new monopoly on authority, many of his allies, most of the provincial samurai, and even members of his own clan turned against him. [[:en:Prince Mochihito|Prince Mochihito]], brother of [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]], called on Kiyomori's old rivals of the Minamoto clan to rise against the Taira, beginning the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] in the middle of 1180. Kiyomori died early the next year from sickness, leaving his son [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Munemori]] to preside over the downfall and destruction of the Taira at the hands of the Minamoto in 1185.{{rp|278,287}} ''The Tale of the Heike'' states that as he lay dying, Kiyomori's fever was so high that anyone who attempted to even get near him would be burned by the heat.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=9780231138031 |page=66}} [[File:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|thumb|upright=0.85|left|[[Taira no Kiyomori]] encounters the fox goddess Kiko Tennō ([[Dakini|Dakiniten]]), by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]]] The rapid rise of certain notable figures to prominence, as well as their decline, have been popularly attributed to Dakiniten. A certain anecdote regarding the military leader [[Taira no Kiyomori]] found in the ''[[:en:Genpei Jōsuiki|Genpei Jōsuiki]]'' (one of a number of variants of the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'') claims that Kiyomori once shot an arrow at a fox during a hunt. The fox then transformed into a woman who promised to grant Kiyomori whatever he wanted in exchange for her life. Kiyomori, realizing this woman is none other than the goddess Kiko Tennō (貴狐天王, lit. "Venerable Fox [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]-King", i.e. Dakiniten), spared her life. He subsequently became a devotee of the goddess, despite his awareness that the benefits obtained through the Dakiniten rite (吒天の法, ''Daten no hō'') would not be passed on to his progeny. The story thus attributes both Kiyomori's rise in power and the subsequent fall of [[Taira clan|his clan]] to his performance of the Dakiniten ritual.{{sfnp|Faure|2015b|pp=121, 224–225}}{{sfnp|Bathgate|2004|p=154}}{{sfnp|Smyers|1999|p=84}} ==Cultural references== Taira no Kiyomori is the main character in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] epic, the ''[[:en:Tale of Heike|Tale of Heike]]''. The [[:en:Daiei Film|Daiei Film]] production of [[:en:Kenji Mizoguchi|Kenji Mizoguchi]]'s 1955 film ''[[:en:Shin Heike Monogatari (film)|Shin Heike Monogatari]]'' (variously translated as ''[[Taira clan|Taira Clan]] Saga, Tales of the Taira Clan'', and ''The Sacrilegious Hero'') credits its story as "from the novel by [[:en:Yoshikawa Eiji|Yoshikawa Eiji]]", which in turn is a 1950 retelling of the 14th-century epic ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. The opening introduction to the film, in its English subtitles, is {{quote|Japan, in the Tenth and Eleventh centuries, was virtually controlled by the [[Fujiwara clan]]. But in the Twelfth century, Fujiwara influence began to wane, partly due to the double-monarchy. An Emperor would abdicate but continue to rule from behind the scenes. Thus there was an Imperial Court and an ex-Emperor's Cloister Court, both emperors being descended from the Sun Goddess. Inevitably, there was conflict between the courts. Both began to depend on the warriors, the [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]. Until then, Fujiwara rule had involved little bloodshed. Some monasteries also had their own armies. The monasteries used them to intimidate both courts. Big landowners paid no taxes. Piracy and banditry increased. The Cloister Court attempted to restore order using the warriors of the samurai Taira Clan. Thus were sown the seeds of military governments which dominated Japan for 700 years ''[i.e., [[:en:Meiji era|until 1868]]]''. This story begins in 1137, in Kyoto, ancient capital of Japan.}} Unlike most other tellings, Mizoguchi's film includes only the story of Taira no Kiyomori's youth, depicting him as a [[:en:Hero|heroic character]], particularly in breaking the power of the tyrannical armed monks and their [[Palanquin#Japan|palanquin shrines]], where he says at his father's grave "Father, with two arrows from my bow I destroyed a superstition that gripped men for centuries. The courtiers and priests have tried to have me for blasphemy. But others have supported me, more than I expected. Some of them are lords, too. Father, a greater battle lies ahead. But I remain undaunted. No matter how I am beaten, I shall rise again". The film then ends with Kiyomori approaching an alfresco Fujiwara dance, vowing to himself, "Dance, my Lords, dance. Your end is near. Tomorrow will be ours!"DVD ''Le héros sacrilège'', Films sans Frontières 2004 Taira no Kiyomori was featured by 19th-century [[:en:Woodblock printing in Japan|woodblock print artists]] as an exemplar of guilt and retribution, see the accompanying print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]. The famous print generally known as ''The Vision of Kiyomori'' by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] depicts the actor [[Nakamura Utayemon IV]] in the character of Kiyomori, confronted by the horrific vision of his snow-filled garden transformed into the heaped bones and skulls of his slaughtered enemies. In video games, Kiyomori appears in [[:en:Warriors Orochi|Warriors Orochi]] 2, 3 and 4 fighting for Orochi's army and using prayer beads as weapons. He also makes an appearance in ''[[:en:Dynasty Warriors: Strikeforce|Dynasty Warriors Strikeforce]]'', as a boss in one of the game's Crossover Missions. Additionally, he is the main antagonist in ''[[:en:Harukanaru Toki no Naka de 3|Harukanaru Toki no Naka De 3]]''. Kiyomori also features prominently as a sympathetic villain in [[:en:Osamu Tezuka|Osamu Tezuka]]'s [[:en:Phoenix (manga)|Phoenix series]] in the first half of the ninth volume, ''Turbulent Times'' (retitled ''Civil War'' in English), another [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] epic. Like most villains in the series he desires the titular bird for its immortality granting blood, due to his desire to continue to lead and protect the Taira clan and lack of confidence in his successors, but winds up being tricked into buying an imported [[:en:peacock|peacock]] instead. A character named Lord Kiyomori appears in Book 6, "The Lords of the Rising Sun" in the [[:en:Fabled Lands|Fabled Lands]] adventure gamebook series, where he is portrayed as the Imperial Sovereign Takakura's chancellor, and on the verge of war with the self-proclaimed shogun by the name of "Yoritomo". The 2012 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] was about him.{{Cite news|title=大河ドラマ「Taira no Kiyomori」：語り部は敵方・源頼朝 イケメンをキャスティング予定|work=まんたんウェブ|publisher=毎日新聞デジタル|date=2011-05-12|url=http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110513031118/http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-date=2011-05-13 |access-date=2021-01-27}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Junior First Rank]] (11 February 1167) ==See also== * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Shishigatani incident|Shishigatani incident]] * [[:en:Fukuhara-kyō|Fukuhara-kyō]] * [[Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Siege of Shirakawa-den|Siege of Shirakawa-den]] ==References== |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-5772-1}} * {{cite book |last=Smyers |first=Karen Ann |author-link=Karen Ann Smyers |title=The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship |year=1999 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-2058-9}} {{Daijō-daijin}}]] {{draft categories| [[Category:1181 deaths]] [[Category:Taira clan]] Taira no Kiyomori | image = File:Taira no Kiyomori Portrait by Fujiwara Tamenobu and Takenobu.png | alt = | caption = Taira no Kiyomori | birth_name = | birth_date = {{birth date|1118|2|10}} | birth_place = {{ill|Ise Province|WD=Q864166}}; {{ill|Kyōto|WD=Q740246}} | death_date = {{death date|1181|3|20}} | death_place = {{ill|Kyōto|WD=Q740246}} | nationality = {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}} | other_names = | occupation = {{ill|military personnel|WD=Q47064}}; {{ill|politician|WD=Q82955}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142}} | known_for = | gender= {{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}} | title= {{ill|Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin|2=ja|3=太政大臣|WD=Q1157586|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1157586}}; {{ill|Jugō|WD=Q10895712}} | spouse= {{ill|Q11410130|WD=Q11410130}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokiko|WD=Q3379244}}; {{ill|Takashina no Motoaki's daughter|WD=Q106485436}}; {{ill|Tokiwa Gozen|WD=Q867354}} | children= {{ill|Q106536670|WD=Q106536670}}; {{ill|Q106536766|WD=Q106536766}}; {{ill|Q106549951|WD=Q106549951}}; {{ill|Q11482316|WD=Q11482316}}; {{ill|Q11482410|WD=Q11482410}}; {{ill|Q11483221|WD=Q11483221}}; {{ill|Q11483418|WD=Q11483418}}; {{ill|Q11483437|WD=Q11483437}}; {{ill|Q11486326|WD=Q11486326}}; {{ill|Taira no Munemori|WD=Q2420085}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|WD=Q710537}}; {{ill|Taira no Shigemori|WD=Q1184985}}; {{ill|Taira no Tokuko|WD=Q3315855}}; {{ill|Taira no Tomomori|WD=Q736706}}; {{ill|Takiyasha-hime|WD=Q11565096}}; {{ill|御子姫君|WD=Q11488907}} | father= {{ill|Emperor Shirakawa|WD=Q60949}}; {{ill|Taira no Tadamori|WD=Q3083805}} | mother= {{ill|sister of Gion-nyōgo|WD=Q106448499}} | family= {{ill|Ise Heishi|WD=Q15734686}} | religion= {{ill|Buddhism|WD=Q748}} | native_name= 平清盛 | given_name= {{ill|Kiyomori|WD=Q123817191}} | resting_place= {{ill|Nōfuku-ji|WD=Q11612054}} }} [[Image:Taira no Kiyomori.jpg|thumb|right|240px|Taira no Kiyomori in his later years, in book illustration by [[:en:Kikuchi Yōsai|Kikuchi Yōsai]]]] [[File:Taira no Kiyomori kao.jpg|thumb|right|200px|Taira no Kiyomori's signature (''[[:en:Kaō|Kaō]]'').]] {{family name hatnote|Taira|lang=Japanese}} {{Nihongo|'''Taira no Kiyomori'''|平 清盛||1118 – March 20, 1181}} was a military leader and ''[[:en:Kugyō|Kugyō]]'' of the late [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. He established the first [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]-dominated administrative government in the [[:en:History of Japan|History of Japan]]. ==Early life== Kiyomori was born in Japan, in 1118 as the first son of [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]. His mother, Gion no Nyogo, was wife of Tadamori and a palace servant according to ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. == Family == '''Father''': [[:en:Taira no Tadamori|Taira no Tadamori]]
'''Mother''': Gion no Nyogo (d. 1147)
'''Concubine(s)''': [[:en:Taira no Tokiko|Taira no Tokiko]]
'''Children''': * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Taira no Munemori]] * [[:en:Taira no Tomomori|Taira no Tomomori]] * [[Taira no Tokuko]] * [[:en:Taira no Shigehira|Taira no Shigehira]] ==Career== After the death of his father in 1153, Kiyomori assumed control of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan and ambitiously entered the political realm, in which he had previously only held a minor post. Before that though, in 1156, he and [[:en:Minamoto no Yoshitomo|Minamoto no Yoshitomo]], head of the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] clan, suppressed the rebels in the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]]. This established the Taira and Minamoto as the top samurai clans in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, this caused the allies to become bitter rivals which culminated four years later during the [[:en:Heiji rebellion|Heiji rebellion]] in 1160. Kiyomori, emerging victorious with Yoshitomo (whose two eldest sons were killed), was now the head of the single most powerful warrior clan in imperial capital [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. However, his clan's power and influence in the [[:en:Provinces of Japan|provinces]] at this time is a matter of debate. Kiyomori showed mercy and exiled a few of Yoshitomo's surviving sons, including [[Minamoto no Yoritomo|Yoritomo]], [[:en:Minamoto no Noriyori|Noriyori]], and [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune|Yoshitsune]] – a benevolence that would turn out to be the Taira clan's downfall later on.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan to 1334 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1958 |isbn=0804705232 |pages=256–259}}{{Cite book |last=Sato |first=Hiroaki |title=Legends of the Samurai |publisher=Overlook Duckworth |year=1995 |isbn=9781590207307 |page=111}} Due to his status as the head of the sole remaining warrior clan in imperial court, Kiyomori was in a unique position to manipulate the court rivalry between the [[:en:Cloistered Emperor|retired emperor]], [[:en:Go-Shirakawa|Go-Shirakawa]], and his son, [[:en:Emperor Nijō|Emperor Nijō]]. Because of this manipulation, Kiyomori was able to climb the Ranks of government, though the majority of his promotions as well as the success of his family in gaining Ranks and titles at court was due to Shirakawa's patronage. This culminated in 1167, when Kiyomori became the first [[:en:Courtier|Courtier]] of a warrior family to be appointed ''[[:en:Daijō-daijin|Daijō-daijin]]'', chief minister of the government, and the ''[[:en:De facto|De facto]]'' administrator of the imperial government. As was the norm, he soon relinquished the position and leadership of the [[Taira clan|Taira]] clan, with the goal of maintaining the social and political prestige of having attained the highest office in the land, but being free of the attendant duties. This had been a common practice for many years in the highest levels of ancient Japanese government and in doing so Kiyomori was asserting what he felt was his strong position in the [[:en:Heian Palace|Kyoto government]]. However, many of the courtiers from traditional civil (non-warrior) noble families were less than pleased with both Kiyomori's attainment of power, and how he comported himself with regard to other high Ranking courtiers.{{rp|266–267}} [[Image:Yoshitoshi The Fever.jpg|thumb|350px|right|While suffering from a [[:en:fever|fever]], Taira no Kiyomori is confronted by a vision of hell and the ghosts of his victims, in an 1883 print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]].]] In 1171, Kiyomori arranged a marriage between his daughter [[Taira no Tokuko|Tokuko]] and [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]]. Their first son, the future [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]], was born in 1178.{{rp|268,285}} The next year, in 1179, Kiyomori staged a [[:en:Coup d'état|Coup d'état]] forcing the resignation of his rivals from all government posts and subsequently banishing them. He then filled the open government positions with his allies and relatives, and imprisoned (house arrest) the cloistered [[:en:Emperor Go-Shirakawa|Emperor Go-Shirakawa]]. Finally, in 1180 Kiyomori forced Emperor Takakura to abdicate and give Prince Tokihito the throne, who then became [[:en:Emperor Antoku|Emperor Antoku]].{{rp|275,285}} With the exertion of Taira power and wealth and Kiyomori's new monopoly on authority, many of his allies, most of the provincial samurai, and even members of his own clan turned against him. [[:en:Prince Mochihito|Prince Mochihito]], brother of [[:en:Emperor Takakura|Emperor Takakura]], called on Kiyomori's old rivals of the Minamoto clan to rise against the Taira, beginning the [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] in the middle of 1180. Kiyomori died early the next year from sickness, leaving his son [[:en:Taira no Munemori|Munemori]] to preside over the downfall and destruction of the Taira at the hands of the Minamoto in 1185.{{rp|278,287}} ''The Tale of the Heike'' states that as he lay dying, Kiyomori's fever was so high that anyone who attempted to even get near him would be burned by the heat.{{Cite book |translator=Burton Watson |title=The Tales of the Heike |publisher=Columbia University Press |year=2006 |isbn=9780231138031 |page=66}} [[File:A man confronted with an apparition of the Fox goddess.jpg|thumb|upright=0.85|left|[[Taira no Kiyomori]] encounters the fox goddess Kiko Tennō ([[Dakini|Dakiniten]]), by [[Utagawa Kuniyoshi]]]] The rapid rise of certain notable figures to prominence, as well as their decline, have been popularly attributed to Dakiniten. A certain anecdote regarding the military leader [[Taira no Kiyomori]] found in the ''[[:en:Genpei Jōsuiki|Genpei Jōsuiki]]'' (one of a number of variants of the ''[[:en:Heike Monogatari|Heike Monogatari]]'') claims that Kiyomori once shot an arrow at a fox during a hunt. The fox then transformed into a woman who promised to grant Kiyomori whatever he wanted in exchange for her life. Kiyomori, realizing this woman is none other than the goddess Kiko Tennō (貴狐天王, lit. "Venerable Fox [[:en:Deva (Buddhism)|Deva]]-King", i.e. Dakiniten), spared her life. He subsequently became a devotee of the goddess, despite his awareness that the benefits obtained through the Dakiniten rite (吒天の法, ''Daten no hō'') would not be passed on to his progeny. The story thus attributes both Kiyomori's rise in power and the subsequent fall of [[Taira clan|his clan]] to his performance of the Dakiniten ritual.{{sfnp|Faure|2015b|pp=121, 224–225}}{{sfnp|Bathgate|2004|p=154}}{{sfnp|Smyers|1999|p=84}} ==Cultural references== Taira no Kiyomori is the main character in the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]] epic, the ''[[:en:Tale of Heike|Tale of Heike]]''. The [[:en:Daiei Film|Daiei Film]] production of [[:en:Kenji Mizoguchi|Kenji Mizoguchi]]'s 1955 film ''[[:en:Shin Heike Monogatari (film)|Shin Heike Monogatari]]'' (variously translated as ''[[Taira clan|Taira Clan]] Saga, Tales of the Taira Clan'', and ''The Sacrilegious Hero'') credits its story as "from the novel by [[:en:Yoshikawa Eiji|Yoshikawa Eiji]]", which in turn is a 1950 retelling of the 14th-century epic ''[[:en:The Tale of the Heike|The Tale of the Heike]]''. The opening introduction to the film, in its English subtitles, is {{quote|Japan, in the Tenth and Eleventh centuries, was virtually controlled by the [[Fujiwara clan]]. But in the Twelfth century, Fujiwara influence began to wane, partly due to the double-monarchy. An Emperor would abdicate but continue to rule from behind the scenes. Thus there was an Imperial Court and an ex-Emperor's Cloister Court, both emperors being descended from the Sun Goddess. Inevitably, there was conflict between the courts. Both began to depend on the warriors, the [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]. Until then, Fujiwara rule had involved little bloodshed. Some monasteries also had their own armies. The monasteries used them to intimidate both courts. Big landowners paid no taxes. Piracy and banditry increased. The Cloister Court attempted to restore order using the warriors of the samurai Taira Clan. Thus were sown the seeds of military governments which dominated Japan for 700 years ''[i.e., [[:en:Meiji era|until 1868]]]''. This story begins in 1137, in Kyoto, ancient capital of Japan.}} Unlike most other tellings, Mizoguchi's film includes only the story of Taira no Kiyomori's youth, depicting him as a [[:en:Hero|heroic character]], particularly in breaking the power of the tyrannical armed monks and their [[Palanquin#Japan|palanquin shrines]], where he says at his father's grave "Father, with two arrows from my bow I destroyed a superstition that gripped men for centuries. The courtiers and priests have tried to have me for blasphemy. But others have supported me, more than I expected. Some of them are lords, too. Father, a greater battle lies ahead. But I remain undaunted. No matter how I am beaten, I shall rise again". The film then ends with Kiyomori approaching an alfresco Fujiwara dance, vowing to himself, "Dance, my Lords, dance. Your end is near. Tomorrow will be ours!"DVD ''Le héros sacrilège'', Films sans Frontières 2004 Taira no Kiyomori was featured by 19th-century [[:en:Woodblock printing in Japan|woodblock print artists]] as an exemplar of guilt and retribution, see the accompanying print by [[:en:Yoshitoshi|Yoshitoshi]]. The famous print generally known as ''The Vision of Kiyomori'' by [[:en:Utagawa Hiroshige|Utagawa Hiroshige]] depicts the actor [[Nakamura Utayemon IV]] in the character of Kiyomori, confronted by the horrific vision of his snow-filled garden transformed into the heaped bones and skulls of his slaughtered enemies. In video games, Kiyomori appears in [[:en:Warriors Orochi|Warriors Orochi]] 2, 3 and 4 fighting for Orochi's army and using prayer beads as weapons. He also makes an appearance in ''[[:en:Dynasty Warriors: Strikeforce|Dynasty Warriors Strikeforce]]'', as a boss in one of the game's Crossover Missions. Additionally, he is the main antagonist in ''[[:en:Harukanaru Toki no Naka de 3|Harukanaru Toki no Naka De 3]]''. Kiyomori also features prominently as a sympathetic villain in [[:en:Osamu Tezuka|Osamu Tezuka]]'s [[:en:Phoenix (manga)|Phoenix series]] in the first half of the ninth volume, ''Turbulent Times'' (retitled ''Civil War'' in English), another [[:en:Genpei War|Genpei War]] epic. Like most villains in the series he desires the titular bird for its immortality granting blood, due to his desire to continue to lead and protect the Taira clan and lack of confidence in his successors, but winds up being tricked into buying an imported [[:en:peacock|peacock]] instead. A character named Lord Kiyomori appears in Book 6, "The Lords of the Rising Sun" in the [[:en:Fabled Lands|Fabled Lands]] adventure gamebook series, where he is portrayed as the Imperial Sovereign Takakura's chancellor, and on the verge of war with the self-proclaimed shogun by the name of "Yoritomo". The 2012 NHK [[:en:Taiga drama|Taiga drama]] was about him.{{Cite news|title=大河ドラマ「Taira no Kiyomori」：語り部は敵方・源頼朝 イケメンをキャスティング予定|work=まんたんウェブ|publisher=毎日新聞デジタル|date=2011-05-12|url=http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110513031118/http://mantan-web.jp/2011/05/12/20110512dog00m200011000c.html|archive-date=2011-05-13 |access-date=2021-01-27}} ==Honours== *[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Junior First Rank]] (11 February 1167) ==See also== * [[:en:Taira no Shigemori|Taira no Shigemori]] * [[:en:Shishigatani incident|Shishigatani incident]] * [[:en:Fukuhara-kyō|Fukuhara-kyō]] * [[Itsukushima Shrine]] * [[:en:Siege of Shirakawa-den|Siege of Shirakawa-den]] ==References==
